landing.lede: Interactive exercises to take apart UTF-8, UTF-16 and UTF-32 - by hand, no calculator, step-by-step validation. For developers who actually want to understand the bit-level mechanics.
landing.preview_title_tail: A coach that corrects you bit by bit.
landing.preview_blurb: Each exercise is broken into steps. You encode or decode by hand, and the app tells you exactly where you got it wrong - in plain words.
bullets: Step-by-step validation, not just at the end
Errors explained in plain English, not error codes
Code points drawn at random - no end
UTF-8, UTF-16, UTF-32 - encode and decode, with graded levels

coach.step_label: encode UTF-8 · split the useful bits
coach.feedback: The markers (110, 10) are fixed - you only fill the payload. Right size, wrong bits: recheck how you split the code point.

footer.tagline: Teaching tool. No tracking, no ads.

landing.title: Sandbox
landing.subtitle: Explore character encodings by hand, no quizzes. Pick a module to get started.

sandbox.title: UTF-8 sandbox (encode)
sandbox.subtitle: Convert a Unicode code point to UTF-8.
sandbox.input_help: Accepts U+XXXX, 0xXX, decimal, or a single character.

errors.surrogate: Surrogates (U+D800 → U+DFFF) are not valid UTF-8.

step.binary_desc: Code point {cp} fits in {bits} significant bits (padded to the form's payload length).

format_desc.1: Range `U+0000` -> `U+007F` - 7 useful bits, plain ASCII form.
format_desc.3: Range `U+0800` -> `U+FFFF` - 16 useful bits, BMP beyond Latin extended.
format_desc.4: Range `U+10000` -> `U+10FFFF` - 21 useful bits, supplementary planes (emojis, etc.).

step.split_desc: Split by the form's payload-slot widths ({slots} bits).

markers_desc.1: The byte carries the marker `0` (1 bit). ASCII-compatible code point, no continuation bytes.
markers_desc.2: The first byte carries the marker `110` (3 bits); the next carries `10` (continuation).
markers_desc.3: The first byte carries the marker `1110` (4 bits); the 2 following carry `10` (continuation).
markers_desc.4: The first byte carries the marker `11110` (5 bits); the 3 following carry `10` (continuation).

step.hex_desc_singular: Each binary byte becomes its hex value on `1` digit.
step.hex_desc_plural: Each binary byte becomes its hex value on `{count}` digits.

decode_utf8.title: UTF-8 sandbox (decode)
decode_utf8.subtitle: Convert a sequence of UTF-8 bytes into a Unicode code point.
decode_utf8.input_help: Hex (with or without `0x`), separated by spaces / commas / no separator.

errors.odd_length: Last byte is incomplete (odd number of hex digits).
errors.too_long: Too many bytes: UTF-8 encodes a single code point in at most 4 bytes.
errors.decoder: This byte sequence is not valid UTF-8.

format_desc.1: First byte `0xxxxxxx` (high bit `0`) - plain ASCII, 1 byte only.

step.bit_groups_desc: For each byte, strip the format marker (`110`/`1110`/`11110` on the leader, `10` on continuations) - what remains are the data bits.
step.binary_desc: Concatenate the groups to rebuild the code point's binary ({bits} significant bits).
step.code_point_desc: The binary equals `{decimal}` in decimal, i.e. `{cp}` in Unicode notation.

encode_utf16.title: UTF-16 sandbox (encode)
encode_utf16.subtitle: Convert a Unicode code point into UTF-16 bytes, with a chosen endianness.

endianness_desc.big: The high-order byte of each code unit comes first (`Big Endian`).
endianness_desc.little: The low-order byte of each code unit comes first (`Little Endian`).

format_desc.1: Range `U+0000` → `U+FFFF` - the [BMP^Basic Multilingual Plane], which covers most of the world's scripts. Fits in a single 16-bit code unit, 2 bytes.
format_desc.2: Range `U+10000` → `U+10FFFF` - beyond the [BMP^Basic Multilingual Plane], in the supplementary planes (emojis, historic scripts, rare [CJK^Chinese, Japanese, Korean]...). Encoded as a surrogate pair: 2 code units = 4 bytes.

step.binary_desc_1: Code point `{cp}` fits in 16 significant bits.
step.binary_desc_2: Subtract `0x10000` and keep the remaining 20 bits.
step.bit_groups_desc:
Split the 20 bits into 2 packets of 10 bits.
The left 10 bits (high-order) form the high surrogate, the right 10 bits (low-order) form the low surrogate.
Each 10-bit packet represents an integer between 0 and 1,023 (10 bits = 2¹⁰ = 1,024 values). Add it to the surrogate base: `0xD800` for the high, `0xDC00` for the low.
step.hex_desc_1: The single code unit yields 2 bytes, ordered by endianness.
step.hex_desc_2: The 2 code units (high + low surrogate) yield 4 bytes, each code unit ordered by endianness.

decode_utf16.title: UTF-16 sandbox (decode)
decode_utf16.subtitle: Convert a sequence of UTF-16 bytes into a Unicode code point, given the endianness.
decode_utf16.input_help: Hex (with or without `0x`). UTF-16 takes 2 or 4 bytes per code point.

errors.odd_length: Last byte is incomplete (odd number of hex digits).
errors.too_long: Too many bytes: UTF-16 encodes a single code point in 2 or 4 bytes.
errors.decoder: This sequence doesn't form a single UTF-16 code point. The same byte stream can be one valid code point in BE and multiple in LE (or vice versa).

endianness_desc.big: Each code unit is read high-order byte first (`Big Endian`).
endianness_desc.little: Each code unit is read low-order byte first (`Little Endian`).

format_desc.1: 2 bytes = 1 code unit in the [BMP^Basic Multilingual Plane] (range `U+0000` → `U+FFFF`).
format_desc.2: 4 bytes = surrogate pair for a code point beyond the [BMP^Basic Multilingual Plane]. The high code unit is in `0xD800` → `0xDBFF`, the low one in `0xDC00` → `0xDFFF`.

step.bit_groups: Extract the useful bits from each surrogate
step.bit_groups_desc: Subtract `0xD800` from the high surrogate (10 high bits) and `0xDC00` from the low surrogate (10 low bits).
step.binary_desc_1: The 16 bits of the code unit are the code point.
step.binary_desc_2: Concatenate the 10 + 10 bits then add `0x10000` to recover the code point.
step.code_point_desc: The binary equals `{decimal}` in decimal, i.e. `{cp}` in Unicode notation.

encode_utf32.title: UTF-32 sandbox (encode)
encode_utf32.subtitle: Convert a Unicode code point into 4 UTF-32 bytes, with a chosen endianness.

endianness_desc.big: The high-order byte comes first (`Big Endian`). The 4 bytes are written in the natural order of the number.
endianness_desc.little: The low-order byte comes first (`Little Endian`). The 4 bytes are written in reverse order from the number.

step.binary_desc:
UTF-32 always uses 32 bits, regardless of the code point.
Code point `{cp}` fits in at most 21 significant bits (Unicode caps at `U+10FFFF`), so the 11 high bits are always zero.
This padding is what makes UTF-32 simple: no format to guess, no marker, just the code point in its 32 bits.
step.hex_desc:
Split the binary into 4 packets of 8 bits, then write each packet in hex.
The order of the 4 bytes depends on the chosen endianness.

decode_utf32.title: UTF-32 sandbox (decode)
decode_utf32.subtitle: Convert 4 UTF-32 bytes into a Unicode code point, given the endianness.
decode_utf32.input_help: Hex (with or without `0x`). UTF-32 takes exactly 4 bytes per code point.

errors.odd_length: Last byte is incomplete (odd number of hex digits).
errors.too_long: Too many bytes: UTF-32 encodes a single code point in exactly 4 bytes.
errors.decoder: This sequence doesn't form a valid UTF-32 code point. It needs exactly 4 bytes, and the value must be in `U+0000` → `U+10FFFF` excluding surrogates.

endianness_desc.big: The 4 bytes are read high-order byte first (`Big Endian`).
endianness_desc.little: The 4 bytes are read low-order byte first (`Little Endian`). Mentally reverse the byte order before assembling the number.

step.binary_desc:
Concatenate the 4 bytes (reordered as Big Endian) to reform the full 32-bit binary.
In UTF-32 this binary IS the code point - no marker to strip, no surrogate to recombine.
step.code_point_desc: The binary equals `{decimal}` in decimal, i.e. `{cp}` in Unicode notation.

encode_windows1252.title: Windows-1252 sandbox (encode)
encode_windows1252.subtitle: Convert a Unicode code point into a single Windows-1252 (CP1252) byte.

range_desc.ascii: Range `0x00` → `0x7F` - identical to ASCII (and to Latin-1).
range_desc.special: Range `0x80` → `0x9F` - the Microsoft extension: 27 printable characters like `€`, `™`, `—`, smart quotes, ... This is where [CP1252^Code Page 1252] differs from Latin-1 (which has C1 controls in that slot). 5 bytes remain unassigned: `0x81`, `0x8D`, `0x8F`, `0x90`, `0x9D`.
range_desc.latin1: Range `0xA0` → `0xFF` - identical to Latin-1 (accented characters, symbols).

errors.not-encodable: This code point isn't in the Windows-1252 table. Outside the ASCII and Latin-1 ranges, only 27 specific characters can be encoded.

step.binary: Read the byte value in binary

binary_desc.ascii: Code point `{cp}` is in the ASCII range: its byte `0x00` → `0x7F` reads straight into binary (8 bits, high bit `0`).
binary_desc.special: Code point `{cp}` isn't in an identity range. Look up the [CP1252^Code Page 1252] table to find its byte between `0x80` and `0x9F`, then convert to binary.
binary_desc.latin1: Code point `{cp}` is in the Latin-1 Supplement range (`U+00A0` → `U+00FF`): its byte matches the code point value, written as 8 bits.

step.hex_desc: The binary byte becomes its 2-digit hex value.

decode_windows1252.title: Windows-1252 sandbox (decode)
decode_windows1252.subtitle: Convert a Windows-1252 (CP1252) byte into a Unicode code point.
decode_windows1252.input_help: Hex (with or without `0x`). Windows-1252 encodes one character per byte.

range_desc.ascii: Range `0x00` → `0x7F` - ASCII identity: the byte is its own code point.
range_desc.special: Range `0x80` → `0x9F` - the Microsoft extension. This is where Windows-1252 diverges from Latin-1: 27 printable characters (`€` at `0x80`, `™` at `0x99`, em-dash `—` at `0x97`, smart quotes, ...) instead of C1 controls.
range_desc.latin1: Range `0xA0` → `0xFF` - Latin-1 identity: the byte is its own code point.

errors.odd_length: The byte is incomplete (odd number of hex digits).
errors.too_long: Too many bytes: Windows-1252 encodes a single character in one byte.
errors.not-decodable: This byte doesn't map to any character in Windows-1252. Either it's an unassigned byte (`0x81`, `0x8D`, `0x8F`, `0x90`, `0x9D`), or you entered more than one byte (CP1252 = 1 byte per character).

step.binary_desc: Write the byte value as 8 bits.

code_point_desc.ascii: The byte is `{decimal}` in decimal, identical to its code point (ASCII range: pure identity). That's `{cp}` in Unicode notation.
code_point_desc.special: The byte is in the Microsoft extension. The [CP1252^Code Page 1252] table maps it to `{cp}` (decimal `{decimal}`). Classic trap: if you read this same byte as Latin-1, you get an invisible C1 control instead of a printable character.
code_point_desc.latin1: The byte is `{decimal}` in decimal, identical to its code point (Latin-1 range: pure identity). That's `{cp}` in Unicode notation.

encode_latin1.title: Latin-1 sandbox (encode)
encode_latin1.subtitle: Convert a Unicode code point to a Latin-1 (ISO 8859-1) byte. 1:1 mapping over the `U+0000` → `U+00FF` range.

errors.not_encodable: Code point outside the Latin-1 range (beyond `U+00FF`).

step.binary_desc: Code point `{cp}` fits in exactly 8 bits. Latin-1 makes the code point value coincide with the byte value: no marker, no splitting.
step.hex_desc: The 8 bits form a single byte, i.e. `2` hex digits.

decode_latin1.title: Latin-1 sandbox (decode)
decode_latin1.subtitle: Convert a Latin-1 (ISO 8859-1) byte into a Unicode code point. 1:1 mapping, perfect identity over `0x00` → `0xFF`.
decode_latin1.input_help: A single hex byte (with or without `0x`). Latin-1 is strictly 1 byte = 1 code point.

errors.odd_length: The byte is incomplete (odd number of hex digits).
errors.too_long: Too many bytes: Latin-1 encodes a code point in exactly 1 byte.
errors.decoder: This byte is not a valid Latin-1 byte.

step.binary_desc: The byte is directly the 8 bits of the code point (no marker to strip).
step.code_point_desc: The byte equals `{decimal}` in decimal, i.e. `{cp}` in Unicode notation. Latin-1 makes the byte value and the code point coincide over the whole range.

danger.title: Danger zone
danger.subtitle: Irreversible action.
danger.description: Deleting your account erases your progress and history. It cannot be restored.

dialog.title: Delete your account?
dialog.subtitle: This action is irreversible.
dialog.description: All your progress and history will be lost. Enter your password to confirm.

exercise.code_point_input_label: Enter the code point in hex

legacy_badge.ascii_hint: ASCII range (U+0000–U+007F): 1 byte, identical in ASCII, Latin-1 and UTF-8.
legacy_badge.latin1_hint: Latin-1 range (U+0080–U+00FF): 1 byte in Latin-1, but 2 bytes in UTF-8.

finalized.failed: Exercise done with help. Try again without revealing to solve it on your own!

resume.message: You have an exercise in progress - level {level}, step {done}/{total}.
resume.message_max: You have an exercise in progress - level {level}.

utf8-encode.title: Charset Playground - Encode UTF-8
utf8-encode.prompt: Encode this code point in UTF-8.

utf8-decode.title: Charset Playground - Decode UTF-8
utf8-decode.prompt: Decode these UTF-8 bytes into a code point.

utf16-encode.title: Charset Playground - Encode UTF-16
utf16-encode.prompt: Encode this code point in UTF-16.

utf16-decode.title: Charset Playground - Decode UTF-16
utf16-decode.prompt: Decode these UTF-16 bytes into a code point.

utf32-encode.title: Charset Playground - Encode UTF-32
utf32-encode.prompt: Encode this code point in UTF-32.

utf32-decode.title: Charset Playground - Decode UTF-32
utf32-decode.prompt: Decode these UTF-32 bytes into a code point.

feedback.answer.type-mismatch: Unexpected answer type for this step.
feedback.format.unknown-choice: The choice `{got}` is not in the offered options.
feedback.format.wrong-choice: Wrong format. Check which value range the code point falls into.
feedback.binary.invalid-character: You typed `{got}` - only `0` and `1` are allowed.
feedback.binary.too-few-bits: Bits are missing: you have `{got-length}` out of `{expected-length}`. Remember to pad with leading zeros when the value is narrower than the width.
feedback.binary.too-many-bits: You have `{got-length}` bits but exactly `{expected-length}` are expected. Drop the extra leading zeros.
feedback.binary.wrong-value: Bit count is right but the value doesn't match. Convert the hex digit by digit - each hex digit is 4 bits (a nibble).
feedback.bit-groups.wrong-group-length: The group at position `{position}` has `{got-length}` bits, `{expected-length}` expected.
feedback.bit-groups.invalid-character: The group at position `{position}` contains characters other than `0` and `1`.
feedback.bit-groups.wrong-value: Groups have the right size but content doesn't match. Check how you split the code point bits into the format.
feedback.hex-bytes.too-few-bytes: Bytes are missing: you have `{got-count}` of `{expected-count}`.
feedback.hex-bytes.too-many-bytes: Too many bytes: you have `{got-count}`, exactly `{expected-count}` expected.
feedback.hex-bytes.byte-out-of-range: Byte at position `{position}` (`{got}`) is out of range (0..255).
feedback.hex-bytes.wrong-value: Byte count is right but the value doesn't match. Re-assemble the format bits into hex (4 bits per hex digit).
feedback.code-point.surrogate: `{got}` is in the surrogate range (`U+D800` → `U+DFFF`), which is forbidden in Unicode.
feedback.code-point.wrong-value: Wrong code point. Convert the bits straight to hex - 4 bits per hex digit, no decimal step.
feedback.endianness.wrong-choice: Wrong endianness. Compare the expected byte order with the format's native order.
feedback.useful-bit-count.non-positive: `{got}` isn't a positive number. Count the bits that actually carry information.
feedback.useful-bit-count.wrong-value: `{got}` isn't the right useful bit count. UTF-8 reminder (payload bits, excluding markers): `1-byte = 7 useful bits`, `2-byte = 11`, `3-byte = 16`, `4-byte = 21`.
feedback.offset.wrong-value: Wrong value. It's the 20-bit value written in hex.
